subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.1.1 Describe numbers and the relationship between numbers 0 to 100
indicator_description: Use number names, counting sequences and how to count to find out "how many?"
examplars:
1. Count by 1s (forwards and backwards) between two given numbers between 0 and 100; or by 2s and 10s; Identify and correct errors or omissions in counting or skip counting sequences [cite: 479]
2. Count to answer "how many?" questions about as many as 100 objects arranged in a line, a grid or a circle; Show that the count of a group of up to 100 objects does not change regardless of the order in which the objects are counted or the arrangement of the objects [cite: 479]
3. Estimate the number of objects in a small group (up to 100) and describe the estimation strategy used; Select an appropriate estimate among all those given for a group of up to 100 objects and justify the choice [cite: 479]
4. Represent the number of objects in a group with a written numeral 0 to 100. Use ordinal numbers to describe the position of objects up to 10th place [cite: 479]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.1.1 Describe numbers and the relationship between numbers 0 to 100
indicator_description: Identify numbers in different positions around a given number (0-100)
examplars: 1. Display a number chart with numbers multiples of say 4 between 0 and 100 and have learners identify numbers in different positions around a given number. Put learners in convenient groups and give each group a number grid and have them identify numbers in different positions around a chosen number [cite: 485]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.1.1 Describe numbers and the relationship between numbers 0 to 100
indicator_description: Use number names and non-standard units for measuring (lengths and volumes) to count to find out "how long or how much?"...up to 100
examplars:
1. Have learners use their feet, hand-span and referent materials to find how long a table, window and door frames are etc., by counting the number of times their feet, hand-span and referent materials are able to do this [cite: 485]
2. Have learners use empty containers such as bottles, cups etc. to determine the capacity of other bigger containers by counting to find how much (the number of times) the bottles, cups etc. are able to do this [cite: 485]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.1.1 Describe numbers and the relationship between numbers 0 to 100
indicator_description: Use comparative language to describe the relationship between quantities/numbers up to 100 using place value and the number line
examplars:
1. Use 1-to-1 correspondence or matching to solve problems that involve comparing 2 sets having between to 100 objects and explain how he/she solved the problem (finding which set has more or less, which groups have the same as) [cite: 488]
2. Use the terms "more than", "less than" or "the same as" when comparing two groups having between to 100 objects [cite: 488]
3. Put groups between 1 to 50 objects in increasing or decreasing order and justify his/her answer or explain what he/she did to find the answer [cite: 488]
4. Identify numbers and groups of objects that are that are 1 more or less than a number (for numbers to 100 [cite: 488]
5. Use the number line to compare and order whole numbers from 0 to 100 [cite: 488]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.1.1 Describe numbers and the relationship between numbers 0 to 100
indicator_description: Represent the comparison of two number up to 100 using the symbols ">, < or ="
examplars:
1. Use the terms "more than", "less than" or "the same as" when comparing two numbers between 1 to 50 [cite: 488]
2. Use the symbols ">", "<" or "=" when comparing two numbers between 1 to 50 [cite: 488]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.1.1 Describe numbers and the relationship between numbers 0 to 100
indicator_description: Describe the relationship between quantities/numbers up to 100
examplars:
1. Use one-to-one correspondence, matching or counting to identify whether the number of objects in one group of up to 20 objects is greater than, less than or equal to the number of objects in another; describe the relationship between the two groups or numerals using the terms greater than, less than, or equal to [cite: 491]
2. Build a group that has more than, less than, or the same number as a given set [cite: 491]
3. Demonstrate an understanding of the relative size of numbers up to 100 by: Order groups of 1 to 20 objects and then a small set of numerals between 1 and 20, and justifying the arrangement [cite: 491]
4. Describe the relative size of numbers up to 100 (i.e., say whether one number is a lot or a little bigger or smaller than another, or 5 more than another number) [cite: 491]
5. Place given numerals between 0 and 50 on a number line that has 0, 5 10 and 20 indicated as benchmarks [cite: 491]
6. Act out and solving problems (pictures and words) that involve comparing quantities (i.e., Johnson has 3 mangoes, Adwoa has 7. what can you say?) [cite: 491]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B1.1.2.1 Develop a conceptual understanding of addition and subtraction
indicator_description: Demonstrate understanding of addition as joining and subtraction as separating; numbers 0 to 20
examplars:
1. Acting out a given story problem presented orally (e.g., Sena has 8 bottle caps. She takes 5 more... Kojo has 15 pencils. He gave 7 away...) [cite: 494]
2. Creating a story problem for subtraction or addition or for a given number sentence (+ and - within 20) [cite: 494]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B1.1.2.2 Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of equality
indicator_description: Use objects and pictorial models to solve word problems involving joining, separating and comparing sets within 20 and unknowns
examplars:
1. Explaining that = means "the same as"; Identifying if two quantities or groups of objects are equal or not and justifying answers; Using the symbol = to record equal relationships; Representing a pictorial or concrete equality in symbolic form [cite: 494]
2. Use a symbol to represent the unknown in an addition or subtraction statement [cite: 494]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B1.1.2.2 Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of equality
indicator_description: Use relationship between addition and subtraction to demonstrate understanding of equality for numbers within 20
examplars: 1. Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between addition and subtraction by: transforming a subtraction as an equivalent addition and vice versa (e.g., subtracting eight from 10 is the same as identifying the number that must be added to 8 to make 10) [cite: 498]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B1.1.2.2 Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of equality
indicator_description: Generate and solve word problem situations when given a number sentence involving addition and subtraction of numbers within 20
examplars: 1. Write addition and subtraction problems, learners in their groups discuss and generate word problems to match the number sentences [cite: 498]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B1.1.2.3 Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction- relationships
indicator_description: Use strategies for solving basic addition facts (and related subtraction fact) to 10
examplars:
1. Naming the number that is 1 more, 2 more, 1 less, or 2 less than a number given by the teacher or another pupil (for numbers up to 20 only) [cite: 501]
2. Naming the double of a number to 10 [cite: 501]
3. Identifying 10 more or less than a number between 0 and 20, and eventually between 0 and 100 [cite: 501]
4. Identifying combinations to 5 or 10 [cite: 501]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B1.1.2.4 Apply strategies for adding and subtracting to 20
indicator_description: Use counting on, counting down and missing addend strategies for adding and subtracting within 20
examplars:
1. Relating counting to addition (i.e., recognizing that adding 2 is the same as counting on 2) [cite: 504]
2. Counting on (i.e., for 5+3, start at 5 and count on 3 places... 6, 7, 8) [cite: 504]
3. "Making 10s" (e.g., if 7+2+3, do 7+3=10 first then add 2) [cite: 504]
4. "Making doubles" (e.g., if 5+4 do 4+4=8 then add 1) [cite: 504]
5. Relating subtraction to counting down (i.e., for 15-3, start at 15 and count back 3 places) [cite: 504]
6. Relating subtraction to comparison or finding the difference [cite: 504]
7. "Using addition to subtract" or re-writing as addition sentence and finding the missing addend [cite: 504]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B1.1.2.4 Apply strategies for adding and subtracting to 20
indicator_description: Solve one-step word problems involving addition and subtraction within 20 using a variety of strategies
examplars:
1. Use a variety of strategies (objects, drawings, mental strategies, counting on, doubles etc.) to solve addition word problems to 20 [cite: 508]
2. Use a variety of strategies (objects, drawings, mental strategies, counting down, etc.) to solve subtraction word problems to 20 [cite: 508]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.3.1 Develop an understanding of halves using concrete and pictorial representations
indicator_description: Understand the fraction one-half as the quantity obtained by taking 1 part when a whole is partitioned into two equal parts
examplars:
1. Use concrete objects to explain the fraction half as the quantity obtained by taking 1 part when a whole object is partitioned into two equal parts [cite: 512]
2. Use pictorial representations to explain the fraction half [cite: 512]
3. Use pictorial representations to help learners sort fractions into those that are halves and those that are not halves [cite: 512]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.3.1 Develop an understanding of halves using concrete and pictorial representations
indicator_description: Count in halves using concrete and pictorial representations of halves
examplars:
1. Show several halves of concrete objects (like half oranges, half stick, half card) and have them count in halves (one-half, two-halves, three-halves, etc.) [cite: 514]
2. Show learners several pictorial representations of halves and have them count [cite: 514]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.1.4.1 Identify coins, their values and the relationships among them in order to recognize the need for monetary transactions
indicator_description: Recognise Ghanaian coins by name and value and describe the relationship among them
examplars:
1. Display the various coins currently being used in Ghana and initiate discussion on the need for monetary transaction. Learners touch feel and say the features of each coin [cite: 517]
2. Introduce the one pesewa, five pesewas, ten pesewas, twenty pesewas, fifty pesewa and guide learners to identify them by name and value [cite: 517]
3. State the relationship between 2p and 10p; 5p and 10p; 2p and 20p; 1p and ¢1; 10p and ¢1 [cite: 517]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.2.1.1 Recognize, create, extend and describe non-numerical and simple numerical patterns
indicator_description: Demonstrate an understanding of repeating patterns with 2 to 4 repeating elements
examplars:
1. Identifying, duplicating, extending or creating a simple number patterns, shape patterns, sound patterns, or action patterns with 2 to 4 repeating elements [cite: 521]
2. Identifying and describing errors or missing elements in number, shape, sound or action patterns [cite: 521]
3. Representing a repeating sound or number pattern as shape pattern or vice versa [cite: 521]
4. Identifying and describing patterns in and outside the classroom (e.g., in a song, in a fabric like Kente) [cite: 521]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.3.1.1 Analyse attributes of two-dimensional shapes and three-dimensional objects to develop general concept about their properties
indicator_description: Distinguish between attributes that define a two-dimensional figure or three-dimensional figure and attributes that do not define the shape
examplars:
1. Identify 2D shapes (triangles, squares, rectangles, circles) on the surfaces of 3D objects in the classroom; Identify what features define a shape (e.g. triangles have three sides) versus other features (colour, orientation or size) [cite: 526]
2. Sort a given set of 2D shapes and 3D objects using a given single feature (size, shape, etc.) and explain the sorting rule [cite: 526]
3. Identify 3D objects in the environment that have parts similar to a given 2D shape (e.g., parts of a can similar to a circle) [cite: 526]
4. Create a composite 2D shape from 2D shapes (e.g., build a new shape using triangles/circles/squares) and describe it [cite: 526]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.3.1.1 Analyse attributes of two-dimensional shapes and three-dimensional objects to develop general concept about their properties
indicator_description: Identify three-dimensional shapes and describe their attributes using formal geometric language
examplars:
1. Sort a given set of 3D shapes using a given single feature or criteria (size, shape, etc.) and explain the sorting rule [cite: 530]
2. Describe the difference between two given pre-sorted sets of familiar 3D shapes and the feature [cite: 530]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.3.1.1 Analyse attributes of two-dimensional shapes and three-dimensional objects to develop general concept about their properties
indicator_description: Identify two-dimensional shapes and describe their attributes using formal geometric language
examplars:
1. Sort a given set of 2D shapes using a given single feature or criteria (size, shape, etc.) and explain the sorting rule [cite: 530]
2. Describe the difference between two given pre-sorted sets of familiar 2D shapes and the feature [cite: 530]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.3.2.1 Describe the position of objects in space
indicator_description: Tell the position of objects relative other objects in space using words such above, below, to the right etc.
examplars: 1. Learners tell their sitting position relative to other children in the classroom (e.g., Yaw is on the third line, three places from Ama and to the left of Kwesi) [cite: 534]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B1.3.3.1 Demonstrate an understanding of Measurement
indicator_description: Develop an understanding of measuring as a process of comparing pairs of items using words such as smaller, longer, thinner, heavier, bigger etc.
examplars: 1. Learners bring together pairs of objects on the same flat surface to compare to find out which is taller [cite: 535]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organisation, Interpretation, Presentation and Analysis
content_standard: B1.4.1.1 Organise, represent and interpret data
indicator_description: Organise and represent (using pictures/objects) data with up to three categories
examplars:
1. Have a picture of learners in front of the class and ask the learners to use two different pictures/objects to represent the number of males and females. Repeat with pictures of animals [cite: 539]
2. Using a one-to-one correspondence to solve simple problems (i.e. how many altogether, how many more or less) requiring interpretation of the concrete representation [cite: 539]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organisation, Interpretation, Presentation and Analysis
content_standard: B1.4.1.1 Organise, represent and interpret data
indicator_description: Organise a given set of data into three categories, find the total number of data points and determine how many are in each category
examplars:
1. Learners use tally charts with data relevant to their daily lives (e.g. favourite drinks, eye colour, pets etc) to analyze and compare data in a picture graph [cite: 539]
2. Learners construct pictures graphs in groups as well as individually based on data given them [cite: 539]
